what_it_does: A harvest ledger entry for a fork/session that records tagged claims, typed relations, open questions, dead-end findings, and provenance for three strands: a role/schema/think resolver, a decision-surface backend L1–L5, and a code-archaeology dragnet primitive.
observations: Every claim is tagged verified / attempted-unverified / broken / abandoned plus a WHY.
Three strands: resolve_slot resolver, decision-surface backend, and code-archaeology dragnet.
Honest gaps are explicitly recorded, e.g. L5 propose live route never fired and the operator loop never closed on a real Tim decision.
Claim 5 tracks dragnet milestones M1, M2, M3 with coverage numbers and still-honest caveats.
Dead-ends section documents concrete bug stories and self-caught blind spots.
Relations are typed: enables, composes_with, derives_from, refutes, blocked_by, same_law.
Provenance lists commits, board item IDs, and a design-doc path.
standouts: The default-to-wrong / verify-live discipline and explicit unverified gaps.
The code-archaeology dragnet coverage-correctness thesis and the self-caught git-denominator blind spot.
The binding trap: default_model must be top-level; nested default_model is silently ignored.
The operator L1–L5 loop has never closed on a real Tim decision.
conventions: Claim-level verification tagging with status and reason.
No self-certification; honest not-done beats poisoned done.
Session-attributed provenance: author_session, commits, board items.
STILL HONEST caveats even for completed milestones.
concerns: Some verified claims rely on lead-reported live output rather than fork-fired confirmation.
Sub-gaps inside verified claims could be missed, e.g. model-leg verified but integrated live route not.
Dense internal jargon and external markers reduce readability outside the session.
Board:// and code:// references point to artifacts not explained in this file.
notes: A self-audited build ledger for a resolver/decision-wire/dragnet fork, notable for tagging every claim with live-use verification status and leaving the operator decision loop explicitly open.
questions: What exactly is the turn coordinate and how is it represented?
What are the precise definitions of L1 through L5 in the decision-surface backend?
What is the chat-4b contention and how was it resolved?
What is the code:// marker-keyword and the recollection counterpart?
What do DEFAULT_BRAIN=-pro and the TIM-RULE anti-pattern refer to in the codebase?
purpose_vs_actual: Yes — the file is a harvest/ledger entry that does exactly what it states: it records tagged claims, relations, open questions, and dead-ends for the resolver/decision-wire/dragnet strand.
apparent_themes: Coverage-completeness as an antidote to build-from-partial-info.
Self-caught blind spots and the tool auditing itself.
intention_signals: It tries to create a trustworthy, auditable record of what was actually built and proven versus what remains open, and to establish a reusable code-archaeology dragnet primitive for pre-build coverage mapping.
novelty: The strict claim-level verification tagging with explicit still-honest caveats, and the coverage-complete dragnet primitive designed to kill build-from-partial-info, including the self-caught git-denominator blind spot.
contribution: A worked example of claim-level verification tagging and honest gap labeling; a design and milestone record for resolve_slot, the L5 propose engine, the binding-trap finding, and the code-archaeology dragnet M1–M3; typed inter-claim relations and open questions that a fused system can use to trace verified, unverified, and blocked state.
summary_for_embedding: Harvest ledger for a fork documenting tagged claims about a role/schema/think resolver, a decision-surface backend L1–L5, and a code-archaeology dragnet primitive, with explicit verification status, relations, open questions, and provenance.
intention_for_embedding: To record what was built, verified by live use, left unverified, or blocked, so the next session can resume without false-green assumptions.
